one_line_takeaway: FedGraph-VASP swaps FedSage+'s generative neighbor imputation for encrypted (Kyber-512+AES-GCM) exchange of real boundary-node GNN embeddings in federated AML detection, winning by 12.1% F1 on low-connectivity Elliptic-Bitcoin partitions but losing to FedSage+ on the sparser Ethereum dataset, and its privacy audit shows embeddings resist inversion (R2=0.32) far better than they resist membership inference (AUC=0.95).
key_finding: On the primary Louvain-partitioned Elliptic Bitcoin setting, FedGraph-VASP reaches F1=0.508 (P=0.64, R=0.43), beating Local GNN (F1=0.389, P=0.74, R=0.35), FedAvg (F1=0.499, P=0.58, R=0.45), and the generative FedSage+ baseline (F1=0.453, P=0.90, R=0.48) -- a stated 12.1% F1 improvement over FedSage+. Under a supplementary high-connectivity METIS partition (33% cross-edges), FedGraph-VASP reaches F1=0.633, roughly matching FedAvg (0.629) and centralized GraphSAGE (0.65 per Table II), while FedSage+ jumps to 0.565 -- illustrating that FedGraph-VASP's advantage is concentrated in low-connectivity/high-imbalance regimes and narrows as connectivity increases. On the Ethereum fraud dataset (9,841 nodes, 98,410 k-NN edges, k=10), the pattern reverses: FedGraph-VASP (F1=0.635) underperforms both Local GNN (0.785) and especially FedSage+ (0.855), which the authors attribute to the sparser, more modular Ethereum graph favoring generative imputation over topological exchange -- explicitly framed by the authors as a trade-off, not a universal win. Privacy audit: embedding inversion is only partially successful (R2=0.32, MSE=0.581, Pearson rho=0.585 -- i.e., embeddings resist but do not prevent feature reconstruction), while membership inference succeeds strongly (shadow-model AUC=0.95), meaning the scheme protects feature confidentiality much more than membership privacy. Post-quantum overhead is small: 0.10+/-0.01 ms per-embedding Kyber-512+AES-GCM encryption, 95+/-5 ms for a 1,000-embedding batch (~10,500 embeddings/sec), 2.5x ciphertext expansion, adding <0.5% to total training time; communication cost analysis shows boundary-embedding exchange adds ~18.5 MB/round total (10.2 MB embeddings + 8.2 MB PQC ciphertext overhead) versus 131 KB/round for plain FedAvg. Ablation: performance is robust to the boundary loss weight lambda (0.505-0.508 F1 across lambda in {0.01, 0.1, 0.5}) but degrades as client count K increases (0.522 at K=2 down to ~0.20 at K=8 per Figure 2), attributed to increased graph fragmentation.
core_contribution: FedGraph-VASP is a federated graph-learning framework for cross-institutional anti-money-laundering (AML) detection among Virtual Asset Service Providers (VASPs). Its own stated contributions are threefold: (1) a 'Boundary Embedding Exchange' protocol in which VASPs share compressed GraphSAGE node embeddings (not raw features or gradients) for 'boundary nodes' -- accounts with cross-institutional transactions -- combined with a boundary alignment loss (cosine similarity between local and foreign embeddings of the same account) added to the classification loss; (2) securing every embedding exchange with a hybrid post-quantum KEM-DEM scheme (NIST-standardized Kyber-512 for key encapsulation + AES-256-GCM for authenticated encryption, implemented via liboqs-python) to defend against 'harvest now, decrypt later' quantum threats; and (3) an empirical evaluation on the Elliptic Bitcoin dataset (203,769 nodes, 234,355 edges, 4,545 illicit/42,019 licit/157,205 unlabeled) partitioned across simulated VASPs with Louvain community detection (~0.24% cross-silo edges, described as more realistic than prior METIS partitions), plus a secondary Ethereum fraud dataset evaluation and a supplementary high-connectivity METIS partition (33% cross-edges) for robustness/scalability checks.
threat_model: Explicitly an honest-but-curious adversary model for regulatory cooperation among competing financial institutions: participating VASPs follow the protocol correctly but may try to infer sensitive information from data they legitimately receive. Two threat vectors are defended: (1) Embedding Inversion Attacks -- a malicious VASP attempting to reconstruct original node features from received embeddings, addressed via the non-linearity/aggregation of GNN message passing (not a cryptographic guarantee) and empirically evaluated; (2) Network Eavesdropping -- a passive adversary intercepting VASP-to-server communications, including a future quantum-capable adversary performing 'harvest now, decrypt later' attacks, addressed via Kyber-512 + AES-256-GCM. The paper explicitly states it does NOT model active/Byzantine adversaries who inject malicious updates, and assumes boundary-node identification is handled by a trusted coordinator rather than a real cryptographic Private Set Intersection (PSI) protocol in the actual implementation (PSI is only assumed to exist, not built or evaluated).
limitations: The paper's own stated limitations (Section IV-O): (1) evaluated on a single blockchain (Bitcoin, plus a secondary Ethereum dataset), not genuine multi-chain/cross-chain laundering data; (2) the primary Louvain partitioning yields very low cross-silo connectivity (~0.24%), which the authors admit makes collaborative learning difficult, and results at higher connectivity (Ethereum, METIS) are inconsistent -- FedGraph-VASP loses to FedSage+ on Ethereum and only ties FedAvg under METIS; (3) Kyber-512 (NIST Level 1, ~AES-128 equivalent) cryptographic assumptions could require future revision; (4) privacy analysis covers embedding inversion and membership inference but explicitly not 'boundary node linkage attacks'; the membership-inference AUC of 0.95 indicates high real leakage that the paper only partially mitigates in discussion (no DP-SGD integration is implemented, only proposed as future work); (5) no defense against active/Byzantine adversaries injecting malicious updates; (6) the boundary-node identification step assumes a trusted coordinator in the actual simulation -- the PSI protocol needed to do this without a trusted party is assumed to exist but not implemented or evaluated. Additionally (not stated by the authors but evident from the data): the arXiv identifier (2601.17935, dated 25 Jan 2026) and improvement margins are narrow (e.g., FedGraph-VASP vs. FedAvg is 0.508 vs. 0.499, within likely overlapping variance given only 5 seeds), and the F1 gains over FedAvg specifically (rather than over FedSage+) are marginal in most settings, so the practical case for the added PQC/embedding-exchange complexity rests mainly on the Louvain low-connectivity FedSage+ comparison.

datasets_used: Elliptic Bitcoin Dataset (203,769 transaction nodes, 234,355 directed edges; 4,545 illicit/2.23%, 42,019 licit, 157,205 unlabeled)
Ethereum Fraud Detection Dataset (Kaggle/Vagifa; 9,841 nodes, 98,410 edges constructed via k-NN with k=10)
quantitative_results: Louvain partitioning main result: FedGraph-VASP F1=0.508 (P=0.64,R=0.43) vs FedAvg F1=0.499 (P=0.58,R=0.45) vs FedSage+ F1=0.453 (P=0.90,R=0.48) vs Local GNN F1=0.389 (P=0.74,R=0.35)
FedGraph-VASP outperforms FedSage+ by 12.1% F1 (0.508 vs 0.453) under Louvain partitioning
Published-benchmark comparison (Table II): centralized Random Forest F1=0.80, centralized GCN F1=0.61, centralized GraphSAGE F1=0.65, all with full data access
METIS high-connectivity partition (33% cross-edges): FedGraph-VASP F1=0.63 (P=0.80,R=0.51), FedAvg F1=0.62 (P=0.78,R=0.51), FedSage+ F1=0.55 (P=0.91,R=0.37), Local GNN F1=0.48 (P=0.94,R=0.32)
Ethereum dataset: Local GNN F1=0.785, FedAvg F1=0.640, FedSage+ F1=0.855, FedGraph-VASP F1=0.635
Ablation on boundary loss weight lambda: 0.505 (lambda=0.01, -0.9%), 0.508 (lambda=0.1, baseline), 0.507 (lambda=0.5, -0.6%)
Ablation on client count K: F1=0.522 (K=2, +2.8%), 0.508 (K=3, baseline), degrading toward ~0.20 (K=8, per Fig. 2)
Privacy audit: embedding inversion R2=0.32, MSE=0.581, Pearson rho=0.585; membership inference (shadow models) AUC=0.95

PQC overhead: 0.10+/-0.01 ms per-embedding encryption latency; 95+/-5 ms per 1,000-embedding batch; throughput ~10,500 embeddings/sec; ciphertext expansion ratio 2.5x; Kyber-512 public key 800 bytes, ciphertext 768 bytes

Communication cost per round: FedAvg 131 KB total vs FedGraph-VASP 18.5 MB total (10.2 MB boundary embeddings + 8.2 MB PQC ciphertext overhead), based on 128-dim embeddings, ~20K boundary nodes, 3 clients
baselines_compared: FedAvg (McMahan et al. 2017 -- standard federated parameter averaging, no structural exchange)
FedSage+ (Zhang et al. 2021, NeurIPS -- generative missing-neighbor imputation for subgraph federated learning)
Centralized Random Forest, GCN, and GraphSAGE (published Elliptic benchmarks with full data access, from Weber et al. 2019 and Hamilton et al. 2017)
